document_metadata.confidence_calibration: Uncalibrated - will adjust after 10+ pilot analyses

findings.title: Ambiguous Ownership of Derivative Intellectual Property
findings.explanation:
Section 9.2 LLM Framework — Contractor IP: Any enhancements, adaptations, or derivatives of the Contractor IP developed during this engagement shall be addressed in the Second SOW or a separate IP agreement, should the parties elect to proceed with formal integration.

vs

Section 2.1 LLM Assessment Deep Dive & Strategic Roadmap: ...the Company will support the Contractor with drafting a high-level roadmap for advancing the build, inclusive of a testing strategy; the Company would provide the infrastructure, data and testing environment to complete.

Problem: The SOW states that the Company will provide significant resources (infrastructure, data, testing environment) to help the Contractor develop 'enhancements, adaptations, or derivatives' of the Contractor's pre-existing IP. However, it explicitly defers determining ownership of these valuable new works to a potential future agreement. If no Second SOW is executed, there is no provision governing who owns the valuable IP created with Company resources during the trial period. This creates a high risk of a significant legal dispute over ownership of the core asset being developed.

findings.title: Contradictory Statements on Cash Compensation
findings.explanation:
Section 6.3 Independent Contractor — No Base Salary: For the avoidance of doubt, no base salary, hourly rate, or guaranteed cash payment is included in this SOW during the Trial Period.

vs

EXHIBIT A — COMPENSATION SCHEDULE: Milestone: $1M raised + onboarding initiated, Bonus Amount: $30,000 (max). Milestone: 5 of 10 LOIs signed, Bonus Amount: $10,000.

Problem: Section 6.3 makes an absolute statement that 'no... cash payment is included in this SOW'. This directly contradicts Section 3 and Exhibit A, which explicitly list multiple cash incentive bonuses totaling over $40,000. While the likely intent was 'no guaranteed salary', the literal language is contradictory and could be exploited by the Company to refuse payment of earned bonuses, arguing that the contract explicitly forbids cash payments.

findings.title: Unresolved Incentive Compensation Amount
findings.explanation:
Section 3 Milestones & Deliverables Table: (Milestone: Contracts executed — early adopter cohort) Compensation Trigger: 3% of up-front revenue captured for early adopter cohort

vs

EXHIBIT A — COMPENSATION SCHEDULE: (Milestone: Signed contracts w/ monetary payment) Bonus Amount: $__________

Problem: The main body of the SOW defines a key bonus as a percentage of revenue, but the corresponding entry in the formal Compensation Schedule (Exhibit A) is a blank field for a fixed dollar amount. This indicates a failure to agree on a critical term. A percentage-based amount is fundamentally different from a fixed amount, and the blank space makes the bonus legally unenforceable as written in the Exhibit, creating a guaranteed payment dispute.

findings.title: Undefined Technical Resource Commitment
findings.explanation:
Section 2.1 LLM Assessment Deep Dive & Strategic Roadmap: At the conclusion of month two, company will allocate a % of our primary technical workstream to support development of the roadmap...

vs

N/A

Problem: The Company's obligation to provide technical resources for the development of the Contractor's IP is defined as 'a %'. This is critically ambiguous and unenforceable. The Company could allocate 0.1% of its workstream and claim to have fulfilled its obligation, leaving the Contractor without the necessary support to achieve the project goals. This undermines the 'value-exchange' premise of the agreement.

findings.title: Contradictory Engagement Start & End Dates
findings.explanation:
ENGAGEMENT OVERVIEW: Effective Date: 3/30/26 | Initial Term: Three (3) months — Trial Period ending 6/30/26

vs

Section 5.1 Trial Period: The initial term of this engagement ('Trial Period') commences on [START DATE] and runs for three (3) months, through [END DATE].

Problem: The document lists specific start and end dates in the overview section but uses generic placeholders like '[START DATE]' and '[END DATE]' in the main body (Section 5.1). This creates a direct contradiction. If the signature date differs from 3/30/26, it could lead to confusion about when the three-month term officially begins and ends, which in turn affects milestone deadlines.

findings.title: Ambiguous Definition of 'Earned' Bonus Upon Termination
findings.explanation:
Section 10. Termination: In the event of termination by [Company Name] without cause, the Contractor shall retain any milestone bonuses earned through the date of termination.

vs

EXHIBIT A — COMPENSATION SCHEDULE: ($1M raised milestone) Payment Timing: Within 30 days of verified capital raise

Problem: The agreement is unclear whether a bonus is 'earned' upon achievement of the success metric or upon the date the payment is due. For example, if the $1M capital raise is verified on June 8th but the Company terminates the Contractor on June 9th, the payment is not due for another 29 days. The Company could argue the bonus was not fully 'earned' because the payment date had not arrived, creating a dispute over a significant payment.
